label: Buddhist Madhyamaka (Indian, Nāgārjunian)

scope.school: Madhyamaka (Middle Way school), founded on Nāgārjuna's Mūlamadhyamakakārikā
scope.period: c. 150–250 CE (Nāgārjuna), with later Indian commentarial development
limitations: Draft authored for schema validation (D1-002); not yet domain-reviewed.
Later Tibetan and East Asian Madhyamaka interpretations (e.g., the Svātantrika–Prāsaṅgika distinction, Tsongkhapa's reading) are out of scope.

No claims are recorded for origin, cosmic_structure, time, human_place, or fundamental_problem: the pan-Buddhist cosmological background (realms, rebirth, saṃsāra as suffering) is inherited rather than argued in the Madhyamaka texts sourced here. Absence means unspecified, not denial.

claims.claim: All phenomena are empty (śūnya) of svabhāva — intrinsic nature or independent essence; nothing exists ultimately in its own right.
Emptiness is itself empty: the Madhyamaka analysis applies to emptiness too, so emptiness is not a substantially existent absolute or metaphysical ground.
claims.notes: The claim is textually secure; its import is contested among modern interpreters — 'Although perhaps most frequently characterized by modern interpreters as a Buddhist version of skepticism, Madhyamaka arguably develops metaphysical concerns' (Arnold).

claims.claim: No entity whatsoever is non-empty — 'there is no dharma whatsoever that is not empty' (MMK 24.19); entities exist conventionally, as dependent designations, not ultimately.

Whatever exists arises only in mutual dependence (pratītyasamutpāda) — 'there is no dharma whatsoever that is not dependently originated' — so there are no intrinsically existent causes, effects, or agents.

There is no independently existent self: the self depends on the five constituents (skandhas), 'which rules out the assumption that any independently existent substance could be regarded as a self'; persons are conventionally real.

Liberation comes through understanding the ultimate by way of the conventional: 'without relying on the conventional truth, the meaning of the ultimate cannot be explained, and without understanding the meaning of the ultimate, nirvāṇa is not achieved' (MMK 24.10).
Ultimately, saṃsāra and nirvāṇa are not different: 'There is, on the part of saṃsāra, no difference at all from nirvāṇa' (MMK 25.19).
claims.notes: The verse is explicit; what the non-difference amounts to is a long-standing locus of commentarial and scholarly dispute.

claims.claim: Cosmological knowledge is structured by two truths — 'the Dharma taught by the buddhas is precisely based on the two truths' (MMK 24.8): conventional truth grounds ordinary epistemic practice, while knowledge of the ultimate 'takes our minds beyond the bounds of conceptual and linguistic conventions'.
Madhyamaka advances no philosophical thesis (pratijñā) of its own; its arguments function therapeutically, undermining conceptual proliferation rather than establishing doctrines.
claims.notes: Whether the no-thesis stance is self-consistent, and whether Madhyamaka is best read as skepticism, semantics, or metaphysics, is contested among interpreters.

distinguishing_claims: Emptiness of all phenomena, including emptiness itself
Ultimate non-difference of saṃsāra and nirvāṇa
common_confusions: Emptiness is not nihilism: conventional reality is affirmed, not denied.
'Empty' means empty of intrinsic nature (svabhāva), not nonexistent.
Madhyamaka is one Buddhist school among several; this card does not describe Buddhism as a whole.

sources.title: Nāgārjuna (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: Jan Christoph Westerhoff
sources.passage: Emptiness is of course always the emptiness of something, and the something Nāgārjuna has in mind here is svabhāva. [...] The self is obviously seen as depending on the five constituents, which rules out the assumption that any independently existent substance could be regarded as a self. (First published 2010-02-10; substantive revision 2026-05-31.)

sources.title: The Theory of Two Truths in India (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: Sonam Thakchoe
sources.passage: the Dharma taught by the buddhas is precisely based on the two truths: a truth of mundane conventions and a truth of the ultimate (MMK 24:8). [...] without relying on the conventional truth, the meaning of the ultimate cannot be explained, and without understanding the meaning of the ultimate, nirvāṇa is not achieved (MMK 24.10). [...] Knowledge of the ultimate truth [...] takes our minds beyond the bounds of conceptual and linguistic conventions. (First published 2011-02-17; substantive revision 2022-05-16.)

sources.title: Madhyamaka Buddhist Philosophy (Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: Dan Arnold
sources.passage: Since there is no dharma whatsoever that is not dependently originated, therefore there is no dharma whatsoever that is not empty. (MMK 24.19) [...] 'emptiness' itself is empty — the Mādhyamika analysis is to be applied not only to all existents, but also to this analysis thereof. [...] There is, on the part of saṃsāra, no difference at all from nirvāṇa. (MMK 25.19) [...] Although perhaps most frequently characterized by modern interpreters as a Buddhist version of skepticism, Madhyamaka arguably develops metaphysical concerns.

review.notes: Drafted for schema validation (D1-002). All source URLs were fetched and passages captured on 2026-07-06. Awaiting human domain review before any scholarly authority is claimed.
